mission.title: Install and Configure HashiCorp Vault on Kubernetes
mission.description: HashiCorp Vault is the industry-standard tool for secrets management, encryption-as-a-service, and identity-based access. The official Helm chart supports four deployment modes: dev (single pod, in-memory storage, auto-unsealed for evaluation), standalone (single pod with file storage, manual init and unseal), HA (3+ replicas with Raft integrated storage and manual init and unseal), and external (point at an existing Vault cluster). This mission walks through dev mode as the bootstrap install path, points users at HA + Raft for production, and covers the auto-unseal patterns (cloud KMS, transit-seal) that production deployments need to avoid manual unseal at every restart.

steps.title: Add the HashiCorp Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official HashiCorp Helm chart repository to your local Helm configuration.
```bash
helm repo add hashicorp https://helm.releases.hashicorp.com
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Vault in dev mode
steps.description:
Install Vault in dev mode for a fast bootstrap install. Dev mode runs a single pod with an in-memory storage backend, auto-unseals the vault on startup, and uses the root token 'root'. The next step replaces dev mode with HA + Raft for any environment that needs durable storage or rotation-resilient auth.
```bash
helm install vault hashicorp/vault --version 0.32.0 \
  --namespace vault --create-namespace \
  --set 'server.dev.enabled=true' \
  --set 'server.dev.devRootToken=root'
```
The dev-mode warning printed by the chart is expected. **Never run dev mode in production: storage is in-memory and lost on every restart.**

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Vault pod is running and the agent injector pods are up.
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace vault
kubectl get svc --namespace vault
```

steps.title: Test secret read and write
steps.description:
Exec into the Vault pod and write/read a secret to confirm the API is healthy. Run each command separately. The dev-mode root token is 'root'.
```bash
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault login root
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault kv put secret/demo username=admin password=hunter2
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault kv get secret/demo
```

steps.title: Switch to HA mode with Raft for production
steps.description:
Production deployments use HA mode with Raft integrated storage. Three replicas form a Raft consensus group, data is persisted to PVCs, and the cluster survives a single node loss. Reinstall with HA enabled and disable dev mode.
```bash
helm upgrade vault hashicorp/vault --version 0.32.0 \
  --namespace vault \
  --reuse-values \
  --set 'server.dev.enabled=false' \
  --set 'server.ha.enabled=true' \
  --set 'server.ha.raft.enabled=true' \
  --set 'server.ha.replicas=3' \
  --set 'server.dataStorage.enabled=true' \
  --set 'server.dataStorage.size=2Gi'
```
Replicas come up sealed. The next step covers initializing and unsealing the cluster.

steps.title: Initialize and unseal the HA cluster
steps.description:
After switching to HA mode, run vault operator init once on the first pod to generate unseal keys and the root token. Save the output securely. Then unseal each replica with three of the five keys (default Shamir threshold). For an automated production setup, configure auto-unseal via a cloud KMS instead of the manual flow.
```bash
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator init -key-shares=5 -key-threshold=3
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_1>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_2>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_3>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault operator raft join http://vault-0.vault-internal:8200
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_1>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_2>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_3>
```
Repeat for vault-2. **Persist the unseal keys and root token in a secret manager off-cluster (1Password, AWS Secrets Manager) before tearing down your shell.** Losing them is unrecoverable.

steps.title: Configure auto-unseal (recommended for production)
Access the Vault UI and API
steps.description:
Set up port forwarding to reach the Vault UI on port 8200.
```bash
kubectl port-forward svc/vault --namespace vault 8200:8200
```
Open http://localhost:8200 in a browser. Log in with the root token (dev mode uses 'root', HA mode uses the token printed by 'vault operator init'). The Vault CLI also works against the forwarded port:
```bash
export VAULT_ADDR=http://127.0.0.1:8200
export VAULT_TOKEN=root
vault status
vault secrets list
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Vault release. This removes the StatefulSet, Service, and agent injector deployment.
```bash
helm uninstall vault --namespace vault
```

uninstall.title: Clean up persistent resources
uninstall.description:
PVCs are retained by default after uninstall. Delete them to release storage.
```bash
kubectl delete pvc --all --namespace vault
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'vault' namespace and verify it has been removed.
```bash
kubectl delete namespace vault
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current values and Raft snapshot
upgrade.description:
Export the current Helm values before upgrading. For HA deployments, take a Raft snapshot of the Vault data so a rollback is possible.
```bash
helm get values vault --namespace vault > vault-backup.yaml
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator raft snapshot save /tmp/raft-snapshot.snap
kubectl cp vault/vault-0:/tmp/raft-snapshot.snap ./raft-snapshot.snap
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Refresh the HashiCorp Helm repository to pick up the latest chart.
```bash
helm repo update
helm search repo hashicorp/vault --versions | head -5
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Vault
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the release to the desired chart version. For HA deployments, the chart uses an OnDelete update strategy so you must manually delete each pod to roll out the new image. Always upgrade and unseal one replica at a time.
```bash
helm upgrade vault hashicorp/vault --namespace vault --version 0.32.0 --reuse-values
kubectl delete pod --namespace vault vault-2
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-2 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_1>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-2 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_2>
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-2 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_3>
```
Repeat for vault-1, then vault-0 (the leader, which steps down before restart).

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that all pods rolled out and Raft replication is healthy.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n vault
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator raft list-peers
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault status
```

troubleshooting.title: HA pods stuck Sealed after init
troubleshooting.description:
After 'vault operator init' on vault-0, the other replicas come up sealed and not yet members of the Raft cluster. They must join Raft and unseal individually before the cluster is healthy. Check Raft membership and unseal each follower.
```bash
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator raft list-peers
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault status
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-1 -- vault operator raft join http://vault-0.vault-internal:8200
```

troubleshooting.title: Lost unseal keys or root token
troubleshooting.description:
If the unseal keys or root token are lost, the data is unrecoverable. There is no recovery procedure — Vault is designed this way. The only option is to redeploy and restore from a Raft snapshot taken before the loss. Always store unseal keys and the root token in an off-cluster secret manager immediately after init.
```bash
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator raft snapshot save /tmp/snap
```

troubleshooting.title: Pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff with 'storage backend' errors
troubleshooting.description:
If Vault pods crash with storage errors, the most common causes are PVC binding failures (no StorageClass), corrupted Raft snapshots, or a configured KMS auto-unseal endpoint that is unreachable. Check the logs and PVC state.
```bash
kubectl logs -n vault vault-0 --tail=100
kubectl get pvc -n vault
kubectl describe pod -n vault vault-0 | tail -20
```

troubleshooting.title: Auto-unseal fails with 'access denied'
troubleshooting.description:
If auto-unseal via cloud KMS fails, the pod's IAM identity does not have permission to use the KMS key. For AWS, the pod ServiceAccount needs an IAM role with kms:Decrypt and kms:Encrypt on the key. For GCP, the ServiceAccount needs Workload Identity bound to a GSA with cloudkms.cryptoKeyEncrypterDecrypter on the key.
```bash
kubectl logs -n vault vault-0 --tail=100 | grep -i seal
kubectl describe sa -n vault vault
```

troubleshooting.title: Agent injector not injecting secrets into pods
troubleshooting.description:
If the Vault agent injector is not adding secrets to pods that have the right annotations, check that the injector is running and the pod's annotations match the expected schema.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n vault -l app.kubernetes.io/name=vault-agent-injector
kubectl logs -n vault -l app.kubernetes.io/name=vault-agent-injector --tail=100
kubectl get pod <consumer-pod> -o jsonpath='{.metadata.annotations}'
```

troubleshooting.title: UI redirects to https on a port-forward setup
troubleshooting.description:
If you forward 8200 and the UI immediately redirects to an https URL the browser cannot reach, you have TLS enabled but are pointing at http. Either disable TLS in the chart for evaluation ('global.tlsDisable=true' is set by default in the dev mode), or import the chart-generated cert into your truststore for production. Confirm what is configured.
```bash
helm get values vault --namespace vault | grep -A2 tlsDisable
kubectl get secret -n vault | grep -i tls
```

resolution.summary: A successful production install of Vault has three Vault pods Running in the 'vault' namespace with Raft integrated storage backed by PVCs, the cluster initialized once with the unseal keys and root token persisted to a secret manager off-cluster, auto-unseal configured via a cloud KMS so pods unseal themselves on restart, the Vault agent injector running for in-cluster secret injection, and the UI/API reachable on port 8200. From there you can mount secret engines (kv, database, pki, aws), define auth methods (kubernetes, jwt, oidc), and integrate Vault with downstream consumers via the agent injector or CSI driver.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add hashicorp https://helm.releases.hashicorp.com
helm repo update
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator init -key-shares=5 -key-threshold=3
kubectl exec --namespace vault vault-0 -- vault operator unseal <UNSEAL_KEY_1>

prerequisites.description: A default StorageClass is required for the Raft data PVCs in HA mode. For production, plan an off-cluster secret manager for the unseal keys and root token, configure auto-unseal via a cloud KMS (AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, OCI Vault, or another Vault's transit engine) to avoid manual unseal at every restart, and budget at least 3 replicas for the HA Raft consensus group. Helm and kubectl must be installed locally.
